segments.text: Yeah, so this is a great question. I can explain a little bit. I'm not going to talk about metal learning extensively in this class, but just to give a very quick primer for people who don't know about it.
This is an example of a paper on metal learning for low resource machine translation.
You can take a look at this paper or not take a look at this paper.
But the reason why I wanted to look at this paper is because it has a good illustration of what metal learning is.

And basically, if we are doing transfer learning from a single task, what we do is we have like a Spanish English machine translation system, and then we find to try to try to be a good Romanian English or Latvia and English system.

If we're doing multi task learning, which also could be equivalent to like instruction tuning, for example, we have French Spanish and Portuguese. We train on all of these and then we find tuned to be a good Romanian translator or Latvia and trans translator.
Whereas metal learning what it's trying to do is it's trying to learn a good initialization that makes it easy to fine tune to try to come up with a model that is good for fine tuning into new tasks.
The way you do this is basically you have two steps.
I'm agreeing to send and so you have a first step where you train the model.
Where you have an update on like data from French, for example.
And then you have another update.
And this is a very informal description. There's a lot of stuff we can talk about here.
We're not going to, I don't have one planned at the moment.

And so you update once and then you update again and you differentiate through this update process so that this becomes like essentially a good initialization for training on other languages or for other tasks or things like that.
Now going back to the original question. The original question is, is there a connection between metal learning in these task vectors.

I'm not 100% sure about that because I think these test vectors are generally created post-hoc. And so they're not like there's no explicit learning step to try to make them, you know, generalize well.
And I think that maybe might be interesting to people. This is a paper that we like literally just put on archives about last week.

And we didn't actually use metal learning in this in this paper. Just because metal learning actually is hard to implement because you need to do this kind of double differentiation and you can become very, very expensive for large models.
But we did something a little bit motivated by metal learning and what we did is we took a pre-trained LM.

And normally what you do is something like continued pre-training on new documents to learn knowledge from the new documents or maybe instruction tuning, including instruction tuning on data on documents about the kind of data that you would want to be answering questions about.
So like let's say you're trying to train on medical language modeling, you might train on lots of medical documents.
But what we did here is we had a step where we trained in advance to get on question answer pairs and documents from another domain.
And then we have a step after that where we train on documents from the domain we want to answer on. So like we might train on Wikipedia question answer pairs and Wikipedia documents.

And then in the second step, we would train on medical documents and we demonstrate that basically this allows the model to do a better job of question answering over these documents that we find tune on over here.

So kind of going back to the metal learning paper that I talked about before the metal learning paper tries to get the parameters in a good space so that after you find tune on another data set, you do a good job with that.
In this paper, our motivation is that the model kind of learns that when you train on documents, you should be able to answer questions about those documents.

And so when you get a new set of documents, it's kind of been a good part of the parameters face to make that easy to do. So if that, if metal learning is interesting on their tutorials and metal learning that I could probably share.
And then if you're interested in kind of like learning knowledge from learning knowledge from continued pre training or something like that, you could take a look at this later as well.
Cool. Any questions about that?
So anyway, I talked about several methods for merging models together.

There's a popular tool kit called merge kit that makes it relatively easy to do this. It implements a lot of the models that I talked about here, including the linear methods, the task arithmetic method and ties.
So I talked about these there is kind of like a expansion on this. So if you want to merge together models, it's relatively easy to do from a software standpoint.
Another really simple thing is distilling ensembles. And so we already talked about distillation. The idea is simple.
So parameter averaging only really works for models within the same run, same model architecture, same initialization.
So knowledge distillation trains a model to copy the ensemble. And so it tries to match the distribution over the predicted words for an ensemble.

And so this allows the model to make the same, you know, good prediction and so see ensemble make the same bad predictions as ensemble. It just allows you to learn more efficiently, just like distillation does in general.
And actually model distillation, the original motivation for it when Jeff and then I proposed it in 2015 and in this paper was to copy an ensemble.
And use it for a lot of other things like in the distillation, like we talked about in the distillation class, but this was the original.
Next, I'll move on to sparse mixture of experts models. And this is really important. This is used in a lot of modern models. It's allegedly used in GPT4.
And it is definitely used in mixed role, which is kind of one of the same that are open models. So I think it's good thing to know about.
What these do is they take advantage of sparse computation. So if you think about what happens when you do a scalar tensor multiply, where the scalar is zero.
And so the result of the entire resulting tensor is guaranteed to be zero. And so you don't even need to do the computation, you don't need to even bother.
And so this manifests itself in a bunch of different places in modern models.
The first one could be single rows in a matrix multiply. So if you have a big matrix multiply like this.
And some of the rows are zero. Then that's one place where it happens.
You can also do this between zero and not just rows, but also larger tensors.
